1: Thus says the LORD to his anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I have grasped, to subdue nations before him. A pagan king receives the title mashiach — anointed — making Cyrus the only non-Israelite in Scripture explicitly called YHWH's messiah. He is a type, not the fulfillment: where Cyrus frees physical exiles for earthly return, the true Anointed One (the Christ, the Messiah) frees humanity from the exile of sin for return to the Father. The grasped right hand of Cyrus anticipates Isaiah 53:10-11 where YHWH's own hand accomplishes what no pagan king ultimately can: the willing offering of the Servant who makes many righteous.
2: I will go before you and level the exalted places; I will break in pieces the doors of bronze and cut through the bars of iron. YHWH going before his agent — the divine vanguard-pattern that runs from the Exodus pillar of cloud through the Good Shepherd who goes before his sheep (John 10:4). The leveling of exalted places and the breaking of bronze doors is the language of liberation. Hebrews 2:14-15: Christ destroyed the one who has the power of death, and delivered all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery. The bronze doors that imprisoned the exiles are the death-doors that Christ breaks through at the resurrection.
3: I will give you the treasures of darkness and the hoards in secret places, that you may know that it is I, the LORD, the God of Israel, who call you by your name. Hidden treasures given so that the recipient may know YHWH. Colossians 2:3: in him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge. The treasures of darkness that YHWH reveals to Cyrus become, in Christ, the full disclosure of divine wisdom. John 17:3: this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent. The ultimate goal of divine revelation — knowing YHWH — reaches its telos in the knowledge of Christ.
4: For the sake of my servant Jacob, and Israel my chosen, I have called you by your name; I have named you, though you do not know me. Cyrus named before he was born — a pattern that runs through Scripture's covenant theology: JEREMIAH named before birth (Jeremiah 1:5), JOHN the Baptist named before conception (Luke 1:13), and Jesus himself named before birth: you shall call his name Jesus (Matthew 1:21). The naming-before-knowing is the pattern of divine election — YHWH knows and names those who have not yet come to know him. The covenant community named in Christ are those who were chosen in him before the foundation of the world (Ephesians 1:4).
5: I am the LORD, and there is no other; besides me there is no God. I equip you, though you do not know me. The foundational monotheism of the OT — pressed into service as the ground of Christology. 1 Corinthians 8:6: for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist. Paul preserves the strict monotheism of Deuteronomy by placing Christ within the identity of the one God — not alongside him as a second deity. The one God equips his agents, including the one Agent who is himself divine.
6: That people may know, from the rising of the sun and from the west, that there is none besides me. The universal geographic scope — sunrise to sunset — maps directly to the Great Commission. Matthew 28:19: go and make disciples of all nations. Acts 1:8: you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth. The proclamation that there is none besides YHWH is the proclamation that goes to every sunrise and sunset through the apostolic mission — the mission that announces Jesus as Lord (the one who shares YHWH's identity, Philippians 2:11).
7: I form light and create darkness; I make well-being and create calamity; I am the LORD who does all these things. YHWH's comprehensive sovereignty over all opposites. John 1:1-5: in him was life, and the life was the light of men. The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it. The creator of both light and darkness is the one through whom all things were made (John 1:3). The "calamity" that YHWH creates reaches its most concentrated point at the cross — where the darkness covers the land (Luke 23:44-45) and the greatest disaster in history is simultaneously YHWH's greatest act of salvation (Acts 2:23-24).
8: Shower, O heavens, from above, and let the clouds rain down righteousness; let the earth open, that salvation and righteousness may bear fruit; let the earth cause them both to sprout. The rain of righteousness from heaven. Romans 3:21-22: the righteousness of God has been manifested apart from the law... the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe. The salvation that sprouts from the earth is the name Jesus — Yeshua, YHWH saves — which Mary is told to give her son (Matthew 1:21). The heavens-rain-righteousness image is the Incarnation: the righteousness of God descending from above to take root in human flesh.
9: Woe to him who strives with him who formed him, a pot among earthen pots! Does the clay say to him who forms it, What are you making? or Your work has no handles? Romans 9:20-21 quotes this verse directly in the context of divine election and justification: But who are you, O man, to answer back to God? Will what is molded say to its molder, Why have you made me like this? Has the potter no right over the clay? Paul applies the potter-clay polemic to the controversy over whether God may justify Gentiles and unilaterally choose some over others. The answer is the cross: the Potter's supreme work is the vessel that appears broken but holds resurrection life.
10: Woe to him who says to a father, What are you begetting? or to a woman, What are you in labor with? The challenge to divine parenting — who questions what God brings forth? The Son who never raises this question: John 4:34 — my food is to do the will of him who sent me and to accomplish his work. John 5:19: the Son can do nothing of his own accord, but only what he sees the Father doing. The one who has every right to question — the eternal Son — instead perfectly embodies the clay-posture before the Potter, becoming obedient even to death on a cross (Philippians 2:8).
11: Thus says the LORD, the Holy One of Israel, and the one who formed him: Ask me of things to come; will you command me concerning my children and the work of my hands? The Holy One of Israel — identified in the NT as Jesus (Mark 1:24; John 6:69) — invites petition about things to come. John 14:13-14: whatever you ask in my name, this I will do, that the Father may be glorified in the Son. If you ask me anything in my name, I will do it. The invitation to ask concerning coming things is the invitation of the risen Christ to his church: pray, ask, petition — I am the Holy One who answers.
12: I made the earth and created man on it; it was my hands that stretched out the heavens, and I commanded all their host. The creator declaration. John 1:3: all things were made through him, and without him was not anything made that was made. Colossians 1:16: all things were created through him and for him. Hebrews 1:10: You, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning, and the heavens are the work of your hands — cited of Jesus. The one who stretched out the heavens with his hands is the one whose hands were stretched out on the cross: the creator bearing the cost of creation's redemption.
13: I have stirred him up in righteousness, and I will make all his ways level; he shall build my city and set my exiles free, not for price or reward, says the LORD of hosts. The messianic-Cyrus who builds the city and frees the exiles at no cost. Matthew 16:18: I will build my church, and the gates of Hades will not prevail against it. Luke 4:18: he has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives. The liberation not for price or reward becomes the grammar of grace: Ephesians 2:8-9 — by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God. Cyrus frees exiles at Persia's expense; Christ frees exiles at the cost of his own life.
14: Thus says the LORD: The wealth of Egypt and the merchandise of Cush... shall come over to you and be yours; they shall follow you; they shall come over in chains and bow down to you. They will plead with you, saying, Surely God is in you, and there is no other, no god besides him. The nations coming to YHWH's people with the confession God is in you. John 1:14: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us. Matthew 1:23: Immanuel, which means God with us. 1 Corinthians 14:25: when an unbeliever enters the assembly and hears prophecy, he will worship God and declare that God is really among you. The nations' discovery that God is present in the covenant community is the discovery of the incarnation.
15: Truly, you are a God who hides yourself, O God of Israel, Savior. The hidden God — deus absconditus — who is simultaneously the Savior. The cross is the supreme hiding of God: in what appears to be total defeat and abandonment (my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?, Matthew 27:46), YHWH is acting as Savior. Martin Luther's theologia crucis (theology of the cross) is rooted in this verse: God's power is made known precisely in weakness, his glory in shame, his salvation in death. The hiddenness of God is not his absence but his most concentrated presence.
16: All of them are put to shame and confounded; the makers of idols go in confusion together. The shame of the idol-makers. 1 Corinthians 1:27-28: God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong... God chose what is low and despised in the world, even things that are not, to bring to nothing things that are. The cross is the instrument of shame that shames the shame-makers: the idol-system of human achievement and power is confounded by the crucified Messiah who is the wisdom and power of God (1 Corinthians 1:24).
17: But Israel is saved by the LORD with an everlasting salvation; you shall not be put to shame or confounded to all eternity. The everlasting salvation that reverses shame permanently. Romans 1:16: I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes. Hebrews 5:9: Christ became the source of eternal salvation to all who obey him. Romans 10:11: everyone who believes in him will not be put to shame. The everlasting salvation without shame or confusion is the eschatological state that Christ's resurrection inaugurates — the justification that holds eternally and cannot be reversed.
18: For thus says the LORD, who created the heavens (he is God!), who formed the earth and made it (he established it; he did not create it empty, he formed it to be inhabited!): I am the LORD, and there is no other. The earth formed to be inhabited — not chaos (tohu) but ordered for human dwelling. Revelation 21:1-3: then I saw a new heaven and a new earth... and I heard a loud voice from the throne saying, Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. The creator who formed the original earth for human habitation creates the new earth for the permanent dwelling of God with his people — and the dwelling is the Lamb himself, in whom God and humanity are permanently united.
19: I did not speak in secret, in a land of darkness; I did not say to the offspring of Jacob, Seek me in vain. I the LORD speak the truth; I declare what is right. YHWH speaks openly, not esoterically. John 18:20: Jesus says I have spoken openly to the world; I have always taught in synagogues and in the temple, where all Jews come together. I have said nothing in secret. Jesus explicitly echoes Isaiah 45:19 in his trial before the high priest. The God who does not speak in secret becomes the Son who teaches openly in public — the anti-mystery, anti-esoteric revelation who says follow me in the marketplace and on the lakeside.
20: Assemble yourselves and come; draw near together, you survivors of the nations! They have no knowledge who carry about their wooden idols, and keep on praying to a god that cannot save. The summons to all surviving nations, away from wooden idols that cannot save. Acts 17:30: God commands all people everywhere to repent. The wooden idol that cannot save stands in ironic contrast to the wooden cross from which Christ saves: the idol's wood is carried around in human hands, helpless to rescue; the cross's wood is the instrument of the one who will not stay dead. 1 Corinthians 1:18: the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God.
21: Declare and present your case; let them take counsel together! Who told this long ago? Who declared it of old? Was it not I, the LORD? And there is no other god besides me, a righteous God and a Savior; there is none besides me. The only righteous God who is simultaneously Savior. Romans 3:26: God is just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus. 1 Timothy 2:5: there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. The problem Isaiah 45:21 names — how can the God who is righteous also be the Savior? — is answered at the cross: Christ's atoning death satisfies divine justice and provides the basis for justification. God is both just and the one who justifies.
22: Turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth! For I am God, and there is no other. The most universal salvation invitation in the OT — all the ends of the earth summoned to turn and be saved. Acts 4:12: there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved. John 3:16: whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life. The all the ends of the earth becomes the geographic scope of the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19; Acts 1:8). The turning (panah) to YHWH is the repentance Jesus first proclaims: Repent and believe the gospel (Mark 1:15).
23: By myself I have sworn; from my mouth has gone out in righteousness a word that shall not return: To me every knee shall bow, every tongue shall swear allegiance. The supreme verse for Christological identification. Philippians 2:10-11 quotes this directly of Jesus: that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father. Romans 14:11: it is written, As I live, says the Lord, every knee shall bow to me, and every tongue shall confess to God. The NT applies YHWH's own sworn oath from Isaiah 45 to Jesus — making this the most explicit OT basis for the NT claim that Jesus shares the divine identity. The one to whom every knee bows in Isaiah is the one to whom every knee bows in Paul.
24: Only in the LORD, it shall be said of me, are righteousness and strength; to him shall come and be ashamed all who were incensed against him. Righteousness and strength in YHWH alone — and all who were incensed against him coming, shamed, to him. 2 Corinthians 5:21: for our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God. The righteousness that is only in YHWH (v24a) becomes the imputed righteousness that believers receive in Christ (Philippians 3:9: not having a righteousness of my own that comes from the law, but that which comes through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God). Those incensed against him will stand ashamed at the final judgment (Revelation 20:11-15).
25: In the LORD all the offspring of Israel shall be justified and shall glory. The justification of all Israel's seed is the NT doctrine of justification by faith. Romans 2:29; Isaiah 9:6-8: the true Israel is not ethnic but covenant. Galatians 3:29: if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to promise. Romans 5:1: since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ. The final verse of Isaiah 45 is the thesis statement of Paul's letter to the Romans: the offspring of Israel, defined by faith in the Messiah rather than ethnic descent, are justified in YHWH — in Christ — and glory in him.
